issue_title: On some errors journald can close the journal and not try to open the new ones
issue_body:
I've found a strange bug that systemd-journald stopped to write new messages on disk, while leaving the journal files in the corrupted state.
By running it under strace I've found at least one cause of it: sometimes fallocate() (which is called during journal rotation) can return -1 (EINTR). (I don't know yet why this could happen.)
Then the journald prints the error in the dmesg: "Failed to create new runtime journal: Interrupted system call" then simply accepting further incoming events and not writing it anywhere, because no journal files are open.

You can test it with strace fault injection:
`strace -v -s 8192 -y -f -e inject=fallocate:error=EINTR:when=5+3 /usr/lib/systemd/systemd-journald`
Then you can send -SIGUSR2 a couple of times to force the journal rotation.
Then you can check in the `/proc/PID/fd` that no journal files are open.

So I can see 2 issues here:
1) It would be better to handle EINTR or to set-up automatic syscall restarts with the sigaction().
2) Journald could do better when it is unable to create the new journal file. I.e. it could at least try to make it again after some time.
pr_body:
On some conditions (particularly when mobile CPUs are going to sleep),
the posix_fallocate(), which is called when a new journal file is allocated,
can return -1 (EINTR). This is counted as a fatal error. So the journald
closes both old and journals, and simply throwing away further incoming
events, because of no log files open.

Let's restart posix_fallocate() in a case of EINTR.

Fixes: #19041

Signed-off-by: Igor Zhbanov <i.zhbanov@omprussia.ru>
